intro: Converting a backend application from PHP to Python transitions your stack from a web-centric template engine to a general-purpose language ecosystem. This architectural shift is often executed to align server capabilities with modern machine learning stacks, data streaming platforms, or offline processing workflows. The migration trades a stateless, request-bound environment for a persistent application lifecycle.

diffs.from: Gradual typing system supporting scalar hints, with a history of dynamic type coercion and implicit conversions.
diffs.to: Strict dynamic typing with comprehensive, optional static type hints checked via third-party tools.

diffs.from: C-style syntax featuring dollar signs ($) for variables, explicit semicolons, and string concatenation using the dot (.) operator.
diffs.to: Clean, indentation-based syntax using explicit whitespace, standard assignment operators, and formatted f-strings.

diffs.from: Stateless, short-lived memory lifecycle where resources are completely isolated and torn down after every single HTTP request.
diffs.to: Persistent state application lifecycle managing memory via reference counting and cyclic garbage collection across multiple requests.

diffs.from: Historically synchronous and blocking, relying on server backends (FPM/Apache) to handle parallel requests via worker processes.
diffs.to: Native asynchronous event loops using async/await syntax to handle concurrent connections within a single persistent process.

diffs.from: Deeply focused on web tasks, providing hundreds of built-in functions for URL parsing, HTML handling, and database manipulation.
diffs.to: Broad standard library encompassing system administration, networking, math, file manipulation, and data processing utilities.

examples.title: Array Manipulation vs Dictionary Manipulation
examples.description: Converting PHP associative arrays into native Python dictionary manipulations.

examples.title: String Formatting and Concatenation
examples.description: Transforming sigil-based string insertion into modern Python f-strings.
examples.toCode:
def format_user(name: str, id_num: int) -> str:
    return f"User: {name} (ID: {id_num})"

tips.title: Shift to Application Frameworks
tips.body: PHP code often runs directly from files. In Python, you must use application frameworks like FastAPI, Django, or Flask to manage routes and state.

tips.title: Manage Global State Carefully
tips.body: Because Python processes persist across multiple HTTP calls, global variables remain in memory. Ensure proper cleanup to avoid memory leaks.

tips.title: Drop the Dollar Signs
tips.body: When translating code, strip out all variable prefix sigils ($) and transition your variable naming conventions from camelCase to snake_case.

tips.title: Reconstruct Superglobals
tips.body: Map PHP superglobals like $_GET, $_POST, and $_SERVER directly to request payload context objects provided by your chosen Python web framework.

faqs.question: What is the Python equivalent to Composer?
faqs.answer: Python uses package managers like pip or Poetry to download libraries, managing dependencies inside isolated environments using venv.

faqs.question: How do I deploy Python web applications?
faqs.answer: Instead of utilizing PHP-FPM behind Nginx, Python applications use WSGI or ASGI servers like Gunicorn or Uvicorn to host persistent processes.

faqs.question: Is Python faster than PHP?
faqs.answer: For basic web rendering, raw performance is comparable. However, Python's persistent memory architecture gives it a significant edge for heavy startup tasks like loading machine learning models.
